banner: Week 6 · Construction Lab — Perpendicular & Angle Bisectors
essential_question: Can you use only a compass and straightedge — no ruler measuring, no protractor guessing — to land exactly on a segment's midpoint and exactly bisect an angle?
objective: Construct a segment's perpendicular bisector and an angle's bisector using only a compass and straightedge, and verify each construction with a ruler and protractor.

agenda.title: Brief — From Definitions to the Compass

content.heading: From words to arcs
bullets: Yesterday: told parallel and perpendicular apart, and defined bisector, perpendicular bisector, and midpoint.
Today: build both classic constructions with real arcs — no ruler measuring, no protractor guessing.
The test: can your arcs alone land you exactly on the midpoint, and exactly split an angle in half?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather your compass, straightedge, pencil, protractor, and ruler. Practice opening your compass to a fixed width and swinging one clean arc before you start the real constructions.

content.heading: Make them
bullets: Perpendicular Bisector — draw segment AB; swing equal-radius arcs from A and from B, above and below; connect the two arc-crossings with a straight line; label the midpoint M.
Angle Bisector — draw ∠XYZ; swing one arc from vertex Y crossing both rays; swing equal-radius arcs from those two crossing points into the interior; draw the ray from Y through where they cross.
Do not erase your arcs — they are the proof you constructed, not guessed, each answer.

content.preview: Session 3: reason out loud about why the arcs always land exactly on the midpoint, review MUS Lesson Practice 5A/5B, and take the mastery checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can construct a segment's perpendicular bisector using only a compass and straightedge
I can construct an angle's bisector using only a compass and straightedge
I can verify a construction with a ruler and protractor and explain what the arcs guarantee

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W06 Parallel, Perpendicular & Bisectors.md

questions.prompt: Measure AM and MB on your perpendicular-bisector construction with a ruler. Record both lengths.
questions.answer: Any correct pair of equal (or nearly equal) measured lengths, e.g. AM = 3.0 cm and MB = 3.0 cm.
questions.explain: Rubric: two lengths recorded with units (1 pt) + the two lengths equal within measuring tolerance (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Place your protractor where your new line crosses AB. What angle does it read?
questions.explain: The perpendicular bisector is defined to cross the segment at a right angle — a careful compass construction should read at or within a couple degrees of 90°.

questions.prompt: Measure ∠XYW and ∠WYZ on your angle-bisector construction with a protractor. Record both.
questions.answer: Any correct pair of equal (or nearly equal) angle measures, e.g. ∠XYW = 34° and ∠WYZ = 34°, together matching the original ∠XYZ.
questions.explain: Rubric: both angle measures recorded (1 pt) + the two measures equal within a degree or two, and summing back to the original angle (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Reflect: which construction felt more like 'magic' before you understood why it works — the perpendicular bisector or the angle bisector? What do you think the equal-radius arcs are actually guaranteeing?
